title: be going to (do)
desc: 계획된 의도 또는 근거 있는 예측
examples: We've got our tests here and we're going to get started.
Okay, this is not going to reflect well on American education maybe.
I'm definitely not going to SNU.

title: will / shall
examples: How do you think you'll do on today's test?
Okay, this is not going to reflect well on American education maybe.
There's a lot more pressure to do well on the tests.
But it doesn't mean that they can actually speak English well or use English well in day-to-day life, right?

title: can / could / be able to
examples: How about you? Do you think you can beat a native English speaker?
I'm struggling. I can't imagine a high school student having to do that.
I can empathize with these students now.
So usually, if you want to go to a song university, I think you can make it perfect.
But I still can make it correct.

title: may / might (possibility)
examples: Non-value, market value may exceed the economic value of the timber.
So it may come a lot easy, but no, I was humbled.

title: have to / must
examples: So they have to pick what sentence they need to read.
I think it's good to know these advanced vocabulary words for when you do have to take tests like this.

title: should (advice)
examples: Topics should be broader, right?
I should have asked you.
Do you know what it should be?
Should it be Hong's channel?
So I think it's important that if you actually want to communicate with others in another country using English that you should focus more on everyday English, phrases, expressions.

title: should have (done)
examples: I should have asked you.

title: would (habit/conditional)
examples: The only comparable thing would be the SATs and it's not taken quite as seriously.
Of course, the way things are worded in many of these questions aren't like how we would say them in everyday life.
They just try to throw in these big, advanced vocabulary words that no one would actually say in real life.
Let's see if S&U would accept me.
But we would not use it in conversation.

title: Passive (is done / was done)
examples: Of course, the way things are worded in many of these questions aren't like how we would say them in everyday life.
So all of the students that are about to take this test, I'm cheering for you, best of luck, and you got this.
